narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the courage it takes to get this far. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 82% chance of failure for you, which is why your application was rejected—schools rely on this to ensure students are set up for success. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school grade decile, which ranked in the lowest group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing, strongly pushing the model toward expecting bar failure. Your LSAT score of 22.5, well below the average of around 37, was the second biggest factor pulling in the same concerning direction. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, slightly under the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing. Your Year 1 grade decile, also at the bottom compared to peers' averages around the 5th or 6th, added some additional weight toward the failure prediction, though less strongly. Meanwhile, your low family income and choice to attend full-time—common for most students—provided smaller boosts toward a passing outlook. Overall, your profile stands out from typical applicants because your early and later high school deciles and especially your LSAT are much lower than averages, outweighing the positives in the model's eyes. This doesn't define your potential; many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your high school performance trajectory, and seeking GPA-boosting opportunities like post-bac programs or community college courses. Building a stronger academic story could flip this prediction next time—reach out to advisors for personalized prep resources tailored to students from backgrounds like yours. You've got this; persistence is key in law.
